title: -Revenue Grant: FEMA Fire Hazard Mitigation Grant Program

sections.recommendation:
Adopt a Resolution authorizing the City Manager to submit a nonbinding letter of
commitment of matching funds for a Hazard Mitigation Grant application for the City of
Berkeley Home Hardening and Defensible Space Grants Project to the Federal
Emergency Management Agency (FEMA), and subject to its award, to accept the grant,
execute any resultant revenue agreements and amendments, accept any additional
funding allocations from the Hazard Mitigation Grant Program, and designate the City
Manager and Deputy City Manager as the applicant’s authorized agents.
sections.fiscal_impacts:
The City of Berkeley has been deemed eligible to apply for a grant of $6 million as part
of FEMA’s Hazard Mitigation Grant Program for the City of Berkeley Home Hardening
and Defensible Space Grants Project, to provide grants to property owners to reduce
their risk from wildfires. FEMA requires applicants to provide a letter committing to
expend matching funds equating to 25% of the award amount over the four-year grant
duration, for a total project amount of $8 million. The City match portion of $2 million
would include $1,374,296 from property owners who would be required to pay a portion
of their wildfire mitigation costs. The only City funds used will be in-kind expenditures for
fire inspections, and the costs for administration of permit processing, plan check,
inspections and other aspects of the review and approval of the wildfire mitigation
projects. Matching funds of $433,072 for Planning & Development Department staffing
and administrative costs will come from permit fees collected in the Permit Service
Center Fund 621, and matching funds of $192,632 for Fire Department staffing will
come from Measure FF – Public Safety funds, which fund the Wildland-Urban Interface
(WUI) Division.
The project would provide $4.1 million in fire mitigation grants to property owners in the
Berkeley Hills WUI area toward the costs of modifying structures with ignition-resistant
building materials (home hardening) and creation of defensible space. Funding of $1.88
million would offset City of Berkeley personnel and consultant costs for program

sections.current_situation:
The California Office of Emergency Services anticipates disbursing approximately $1.35
billion of Hazard Mitigation Grant Program (HMGP) funding, to reduce or eliminate risks
from future disasters. Berkeley submitted a Notice of Interest for a project to provide
wildfire mitigation grants for vulnerable buildings within the WUI, and was invited to
develop a full application. The proposed project must be in conformance with Berkeley’s
FEMA-approved Local Hazard Mitigation Plan, which seeks to reduce the potential for
loss of life, injury, and economic damage from wildfires, and must help protect and
enhance quality of life in Berkeley, by encouraging home hardening to complement
evidence-based defensible space practices. The project will also encourage residents
and businesses to upgrade the hazard resistance of their own properties and advance
Berkeley’s Strategic Plan goal of creating a resilient, safe, connected, and prepared city.
The new $6 million FEMA grant will help individual property owners make home
hardening improvements and create better defensible space in areas within the High
and Very High Fire Hazard Severity Zone (FHSZ), with a focus on the Grizzly Peak and
Panoramic Mitigation Zones. The City will require property owners to pay 50% of their
total costs, with the remaining half covered by the FEMA grants.

eligible activities at each property and create an inspection report, and Building and
Safety staff will assist participants with applying for grants and meeting program
requirements. Fire and Building and Safety staff will assist with mitigation projects,

sections.background:
Based on CAL FIRE’s recommendations, the City has defined its WUI and FHSZs with
consideration of local conditions including structure type, age and structure separation,
how much home hardening has occurred, and the ability of the transportation
infrastructure to support a timely evacuation. The Grizzly Peak and Panoramic
Mitigation Zones are in the Very High FHSZ, due to significant wildfire risk originating
east of the City’s boundaries, and access challenges. Most of the structures in the WUI
were built over 45 years ago and have not been updated to current Fire Code
standards, and specifically Chapter 7A of the current building code that defines home
hardening practices for homes built in a Wildland Urban Interface.
Since the passage of Measure FF, the Fire Department has created a WUI Division,
which has inspected all properties in the Very High FHSZ. In 2025 the Fire Department
will provide grants of up to $2,900 each, from a separate $1 million CAL FIRE grant, to
assist low-income and disabled homeowners with vegetation management on private
property.
The City has recognized the significant wildfire hazard and recently adopted ordinances
requiring defensible space, with a Zone 0 defensible space requirement in the
Panoramic and Grizzly Peak Mitigation Zones. While home hardening of existing
structures is not required by City ordinance, it is strongly recommended. Home
hardening combined with defensible space has been scientifically demonstrated to
reduce the risk of a wildfire spreading between structures. Staff will prioritize grants for
properties in the Very High FHSZ in the first two years of the program, because these
areas act as a buffer from the significant threat of wildfire originating east of the City
limits. If enough funding remains in years three and four of the program, grants will be
offered to all properties in the High and Very High FHSZ. The City of Berkeley Home
Hardening and Defensible Space Grants Project would provide financial support
sections.rationale:
If the City receives funding under FEMA’s Hazard Mitigation Grant Program, more
vulnerable structures in Berkeley are likely to be retrofitted, the City will retain housing,
safety for residents and other community members will be increased, and Berkeley will
be more resilient in the event of a major wildfire event.
sections.alternatives:
Staff considered smaller grant award amounts to upgrade a greater number of individual
properties within the entire WUI area, rather than prioritizing properties in the Very High
FHSZ. Staff opted to prioritize larger grants in the Very High FHSZ, and particularly the
Grizzly Peak and Panoramic Hill Mitigation area, to maximize the chance of preventing
a fire from entering the City. Having the largest impact in hardening homes and
reducing vegetation in the highest risk area of the city was deemed to be the most
effective strategy for protecting properties.
CONTACT PERSONS
Jenny McNulty, Resilient Buildings Program Manager, Planning and Development
Department, 510-981-7451
Lata Vasudevan, Community Services Specialist I, Planning and Development
Department, 510-981-7491

Page | 4

RESOLUTION NO. ##,###-N.S.
AUTHORIZING THE CITY MANAGER TO COMMIT MATCHING FUNDS FOR A
HAZARD MITIGATION GRANT APPLICATION FOR THE HOME HARDENING AND
DEFENSIBLE SPACE GRANTS PROJECT TO THE FEDERAL EMERGENCY
MANAGEMENT AGENCY (FEMA) AND SUBJECT TO ITS AWARD, TO ACCEPT THE
GRANT, EXECUTE ANY RESULTANT REVENUE AGREEMENTS AND
AMENDMENTS, ACCEPT ANY ADDITIONAL FUNDING ALLOCATIONS FROM THE
HAZARD MITIGATION GRANT PROGRAM AND DESIGNATE THE APPLICANT’S
DESIGNATED AGENT
WHEREAS, as a result of the declaration of a major federal disaster, the State of
California is eligible for Hazard Mitigation Grant Program (HMGP) funding aimed at
reducing or eliminating future damages; and
WHEREAS, federal funding is provided under the Robert T. Stafford Emergency
Assistance and Disaster Relief Act (Stafford Act) through FEMA and the California
Governor’s Office of Emergency Services (Cal OES), Cal OES is responsible for
identifying program priorities, reviewing applications and forwarding recommendations for
funding to FEMA, and FEMA has final approval for activity eligibility and funding; and
WHEREAS, the federal regulations governing the HMGP are found in Title 44 of the Code
of Federal Regulations (44 CFR) Part 206, and the Uniform Administrative Requirements
for Federal Awards are found in 2 CFR Part 200; and
WHEREAS, a Notice of Interest was approved for City of Berkeley Home Hardening and
Defensible Space Grants Project, and the City of Berkeley will submit a full application for
HMGP funding on or before September 15, 2025; and
WHEREAS, a letter committing at least 25% matching funds is required as part of the
grant application, with matching funds from a non-federal source; and
WHEREAS, the match will consist of $2 million over the four-year grant period, $1.37
million of which will come from property owners who participate in the grant program
and make a match of at least 25%
